title: Understanding deconfined quantum critical points from crystalline categorical Landau paradigm
authors: Hiromi Ebisu, Bo Han, Weiguang Cao
abstract: Deconfined quantum critical points (DQCPs) involving lattice symmetries evade the conventional Landau paradigm because the competing orders break incompatible internal and crystalline symmetries. We show that a class of DQCPs can nevertheless be understood as Landau-type transitions after gauging anomalous onsite symmetries. For spin chains with Lieb-Schultz-Mattis (LSM) anomalies, gauging produces a noninvertible lattice translation whose fusion closes only up to ordinary translations, giving rise to a crystalline categorical symmetry. In the gauged description, the original DQCP becomes a transition between different symmetry breaking patterns of this categorical symmetry. We demonstrate this mechanism in microscopic lattice models; the magnetic-valence-bond-solid (VBS) DQCP realizes a Rep($D_8$)-type crystalline categorical Landau transition, whereas a y-antiferromagnetic-VBS DQCP realizes a Rep($H_8$)-type one. Although Rep($D_8$) and Rep($H_8$) share the same fusion rules, they have inequivalent $F$-symbols and therefore define distinct categorical descriptions. Our results show that the universal categorical structure underlying these DQCPs is encoded in the full fusion category, rather than in the fusion ring alone.
summary: The paper develops an advanced theoretical framework to understand Deconfined Quantum Critical Points (DQCPs) that evade conventional Landau theory. It achieves this by showing that these critical points can be mapped onto transitions governed by novel 'crystalline categorical symmetries' derived through a process called gauging anomalous onsite symmetries. The distinction between different symmetry types is rigorously established using the F-symbol of the underlying fusion category.
main_problem: The paper addresses Deconfined Quantum Critical Points (DQCPs) which defy conventional Landau theory due to competing orders breaking incompatible internal and crystalline symmetries.
main_result: A class of DQCPs can be reinterpreted as Landau-type transitions by gauging anomalous onsite symmetries, leading to descriptions governed by crystalline categorical symmetries like Rep(D8)-type or Rep(H8)-type.
method: The core method involves 'gauging' the system's anomalous symmetries and analyzing the resulting effective lattice Hamiltonians in a dual description. The distinction between candidate symmetries is made by calculating the F-symbol of the underlying fusion category.
model_or_system: The analysis uses microscopic lattice models, specifically focusing on transitions between magnetic (FM) and Valence Bond Solid (VBS) orders in spin chains, exemplified by the FM–VBS and yAFM–VBS DQCPs.
key_observables: The F-symbol of the crystalline fusion category is the key diagnostic tool used to distinguish between different symmetry structures ($	ext{Rep}(D_8)$ vs $	ext{Rep}(H_8)$).
important_parameters: The transition parameter $\lambda$ governing the mixing between competing phases (e.g., $bH = (1 - \lambda) bH_{	ext{FM}} + \lambda bH_{	ext{VBS}}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that LSM anomalies provide a microscopic mechanism for generating these crystalline symmetries via the gauging procedure.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the mapping from DQCPs to categorical Landau transitions and use the F-symbol calculation to distinguish between different symmetry types ($	ext{Rep}(D_8)$ vs $	ext{Rep}(H_8)$).
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first identifying the failure of the standard Landau paradigm, then introducing gauging as a resolution mechanism. It develops this using specific lattice models (FM-VBS and yAFM-VBS), deriving distinct categorical symmetries ($	ext{Rep}(D_8)$ vs $	ext{Rep}(H_8)$) based on detailed calculations involving non-invertible defects and their associated F-symbols.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, mathematically rigorous framework for classifying quantum critical points in strongly correlated systems by extending symmetry concepts from standard group theory to full fusion categories. This has implications for understanding exotic phases beyond simple mean-field descriptions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: After gauging the internal symmetry, the DQCP with LSM anomalous symmetry is mapped to a crystalline categorical Landau transition.
FIG. 2: This F-symbol measures the relative sign between two fusion orders:(a ⊗D) ⊗b vs a ⊗(D ⊗b).
FIG. 3: DQCPs with different crystalline categorical Landau origins. In particular, DQCP1 and DQCP2 are captured by Eq. (23) and (39), respectively, while DQCP3 is briefly discussed in the Outlook.

FIG. 4: Graphical representation of the F-symbol. The red, black, and light-blue lines denote the bZσ 2, D, and bZτ 2 defects, respectively. The F-symbol compares two fusion paths, (a ⊗D) ⊗b and a ⊗(D ⊗b), corresponding to opposite orders of fusing the invertible defects a and b into the non-invertible defect D. In the lattice realization, the relative sign is determined by the commutation relation between the corresponding fusion operators.
